concerns.text: The plan proposes using a 'synthetic trait/label generator' to train the model because verified sources for Coral Trait Database and ReefBase are missing. This fundamentally breaks the research question. A model trained on synthetic labels cannot predict real-world bleaching susceptibility; it only learns the arbitrary rules of the generator. The study becomes a simulation of a pipeline, not a prediction of ecological reality.

The spatial hold-out strategy (Train: West Pacific, Test: East Pacific) is invalid if the target variable (bleached) is synthetic. Synthetic data generators typically lack the complex, region-specific ecological interactions (e.g., local adaptation, specific stressor combinations) that a spatial hold-out is designed to test. The model will likely achieve high AUC on the test set simply because the synthetic generator applies uniform rules across both regions, failing to validate geographic generalization.

Permutation importance and FDR correction (FR-007) are statistically meaningless when applied to synthetic labels. The 'importance' of features will reflect the generator's internal logic, not actual ecological drivers. Reporting these as 'statistically validated feature importance rankings' for real-world conservation decisions is methodologically unsound and misleading.

The user story requires the model to 'demonstrate the ability to generalize to unseen geographic regions' and provide 'statistically validated feature importance.' If the underlying data is synthetic, the model cannot demonstrate generalization to *real* unseen regions, nor can it identify *real* drivers. The core scientific claim is unanswerable with the proposed data strategy.
